locked_facts: Equation of continuity: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ (for incompressible fluid)
Volume flow rate Q = Av = constant along a streamline tube
If pipe narrows, fluid speeds up (A decreases → v increases)
If pipe widens, fluid slows down (A increases → v decreases)
Based on conservation of mass — what flows in must flow out
Streamlines closer together = faster flow (narrower effective area)
truth_statements: The equation of continuity states that for steady flow of an incompressible fluid the product of cross-sectional area and velocity remains constant
Volume flow rate is conserved at every cross-section of a pipe carrying an incompressible fluid
Where streamlines are closer together the fluid flows faster
The equation of continuity is a statement of conservation of mass applied to fluid flow
A garden hose nozzle works by narrowing the opening — the same volume of water must flow faster through the smaller area

aha_moment.what_to_show: Water flowing through a pipe that narrows to half the diameter: area reduces to 1/4, so velocity increases 4×. Particle tracers show dramatically faster motion in the narrow section. The volume flow rate Q = Av stays constant — verified by measuring flow at both ends.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Same amount of water per second at both sections — faster in narrow, slower in wide — with Q bars equal
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students don't intuitively connect pipe narrowing to speed increase — seeing the same volume per second flowing faster through less area makes mass conservation tangible

simulation_states.label: Steady flow in a pipe
simulation_states.description: Fluid flows through a uniform pipe. Flow rate Q = Av is constant. Particle tracers move at constant speed. All cross-sections carry same volume per second.
simulation_states.emphasis: In a uniform pipe, flow is uniform: same speed everywhere

simulation_states.label: Pipe narrows
simulation_states.description: Pipe transitions from wide to narrow. Tracers speed up in the narrow section. A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ shown numerically. Volume per second is the same at both ends.
simulation_states.emphasis: Area halved → speed doubled. Mass is conserved — nothing is created or destroyed.

simulation_states.label: Quantitative example
simulation_states.description: Pipe diameter halves (area → 1/4). Velocity quadruples. Q stays constant. Practical example: thumb over garden hose — area reduced, water shoots out faster.
simulation_states.emphasis: Diameter halved → area quartered → velocity quadrupled. Q = Av = constant.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts pipe diameters at two sections. Velocity and flow rate displayed. Streamline spacing shown. Practical: adjust nozzle opening to control spray speed.
simulation_states.emphasis: Narrow the pipe — watch the speed increase. Q stays the same!

common_misconceptions.belief: Water slows down in a narrow pipe because it's harder to flow
common_misconceptions.correction: For an ideal incompressible fluid, the speed INCREASES in a narrower section. The same volume of water must pass through a smaller area each second, so it must move faster. (Real fluids do experience friction, but the continuity principle dominates.)
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show particle tracers speeding up dramatically in the narrow section

common_misconceptions.belief: More water comes out of a narrow pipe per second
common_misconceptions.correction: The volume flow rate Q = Av is the SAME at every cross-section. Faster speed through a smaller area gives the same volume per second. No extra water is created — it's just moving faster.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Measure Q at both sections — identical. Speed up, area down, product constant.
